events.event: The Acts of Union come into effect, joining the Kingdoms of England and Scotland into Great Britain.
The Great Exhibition is opened by Queen Victoria at the Crystal Palace in London.
The Labour Party wins a landslide victory in the UK General Election, ending 18 years of Conservative rule.
Queen Anne Boleyn is arrested and taken to the Tower of London on charges of adultery and treason.
The de Havilland Comet, the world's first commercial jet airliner, begins its first regular service from London to Johannesburg.
The first ever televised FA Cup Final, known as the 'Matthews Final', takes place at Wembley.
The Festival of Britain is opened by King George VI to promote British contributions to science, technology, and the arts.
Margaret Thatcher wins the General Election to become the United Kingdom's first female Prime Minister.
The Polish Constitution is passed, heavily influenced by British Enlightenment philosophy.
Charles Rolls and Henry Royce meet for the first time at the Midland Hotel in Manchester to form Rolls-Royce.
The General Strike begins in Britain as miners and other workers stop work over wage disputes.
The Battle of Tewkesbury sees the decisive defeat of the Lancastrians during the Wars of the Roses.
Amy Johnson sets off from Croydon on the first solo flight by a woman from Britain to Australia.
The birth of Karl Marx in Prussia; his later work in London profoundly influenced British political thought.
The Council of Europe is established following the Treaty of London.
Roger Bannister becomes the first person to run a mile in under four minutes at Iffley Road track, Oxford.
The Penny Black, the world's first adhesive postage stamp, becomes valid for use in the UK.
The Channel Tunnel is formally opened by Queen Elizabeth II and President François Mitterrand.
Germany signs an unconditional surrender at Reims, leading to the end of World War II in Europe.
Robert Browning, the major English poet and playwright, is born in London.
The British ocean liner RMS Lusitania is sunk by a German U-boat off the coast of Ireland.
VE Day (Victory in Europe Day) is celebrated across the United Kingdom to mark the end of the war in Europe.
The Convention Parliament proclaims Charles II as the lawful monarch, marking the start of the Restoration.
The Beatles release their final studio album, 'Let It Be', in the UK.
Thomas Blood attempts to steal the Crown Jewels from the Tower of London.
J.M. Barrie, the Scottish novelist and creator of Peter Pan, is born.
The Beatles sign their first recording contract with EMI's Parlophone label.
Winston Churchill becomes Prime Minister of the United Kingdom following the resignation of Neville Chamberlain.
The National Gallery in London opens to the public for the first time.
The House of Commons is destroyed by German incendiary bombs during the Blitz.
Prime Minister Spencer Perceval is assassinated in the lobby of the House of Commons, the only British PM to be killed in office.
The Bradford City stadium fire occurs during a football match, resulting in 56 deaths.
The birth of Ken Stott, the acclaimed Scottish actor.
Florence Nightingale, the founder of modern nursing, is born.
The coronation of King George VI and Queen Elizabeth takes place at Westminster Abbey.
The birth of Chris Patten, the last British Governor of Hong Kong.
Winston Churchill delivers his famous 'blood, toil, tears and sweat' speech to the House of Commons.
The first ever Formula One World Championship race, the British Grand Prix, is held at Silverstone.
Arthur Sullivan, of the musical partnership Gilbert and Sullivan, is born in London.
Edward Jenner performs the first vaccination against smallpox, a landmark in medical history.
The British Legion is founded to support veterans of the Great War.
The Battle of Lewes takes place, where Simon de Montfort defeats King Henry III.
The present Royal Opera House in Covent Garden, London, opens for the first time.
The first flight of Britain's first jet aircraft, the Gloster E.28/39, takes place at Cranwell.
Anne Boleyn is tried in the Tower of London and sentenced to death.
James Boswell and Samuel Johnson meet for the first time at Tom Davies's bookshop in London.
The 'Dambusters' raid (Operation Chastise) is launched by RAF 617 Squadron using bouncing bombs.
The first Academy Awards are held; British-born actor George Arliss would later be the first UK winner.
Edward Jenner, the British physician who pioneered the smallpox vaccine, is born.
The Relief of Mafeking takes place during the Boer War, leading to widespread celebrations in London.
Edward Stafford, 3rd Duke of Buckingham, is executed for treason against Henry VIII.
Bertrand Russell, the influential British philosopher and logician, is born in Wales.
The birth of Mary Beard, the renowned English scholar of Roman history.
The death of Gustav Mahler; his work remains a staple of the British orchestral repertoire.

War Memorial Unveiling: The Biddenham War Memorial, a Celtic cross on the village green, is officially unveiled and dedicated. it commemorates ten local men lost in the Great War and three from the Second World War.
Anne Boleyn, the second wife of Henry VIII, is executed by beheading at the Tower of London.
An Act of Parliament is passed declaring England to be a Commonwealth.
T.E. Lawrence, known as Lawrence of Arabia, dies following a motorcycle accident in Dorset.
The Battle of Crete begins, involving a large contingent of British and Commonwealth forces.
James Lind publishes 'A Treatise of the Scurvy', identifying citrus fruit as a cure for the disease.
The birth of Joe Cocker, the influential English singer.
The death of Mary Anning, the pioneering English fossil hunter and palaeontologist.
The American Red Cross is founded; it would later work closely with British services during the World Wars.
King Henry VI dies in the Tower of London, likely murdered on the orders of Edward IV.
Sir Arthur Conan Doyle, the creator of Sherlock Holmes, is born in Edinburgh.
The first Battle of St Albans marks the traditional start of the Wars of the Roses.
The birth of George Best, the legendary Northern Irish footballer for Manchester United.
The notorious pirate Captain William Kidd is executed for piracy and murder at Execution Dock in London.
The first Glyndebourne Festival Opera takes place in East Sussex.
The birth of Abdul-Baha; his visits to London significantly impacted British religious diversity.
Queen Victoria is born at Kensington Palace in London.
The British battlecruiser HMS Hood is sunk by the German battleship Bismarck.
John Wesley has his evangelical conversion experience at a meeting in Aldersgate Street, London.
Oscar Wilde is convicted of 'gross indecency' and sentenced to two years' hard labour.
Celtic become the first British club to win the European Cup, defeating Inter Milan in Lisbon.
The birth of Sir Ian McKellen, the acclaimed English actor.
Operation Dynamo, the evacuation of Allied soldiers from the beaches of Dunkirk, begins.
The first ever 24 Hours of Le Mans race is held; British cars and drivers would become icons of the event.
The birth of Peter Cushing, the famous English actor.
The German battleship Bismarck is sunk by the Royal Navy in the North Atlantic.
The birth of Arnold Bennett, the prolific English novelist.
Margaret Pole, Countess of Salisbury, is executed by Henry VIII.
Ian Fleming, the creator of James Bond, is born in London.
Sir Francis Chichester arrives in Plymouth after completing the first solo circumnavigation of the globe via the great capes.
The birth of William Pitt the Younger, the youngest ever British Prime Minister.
Oak Apple Day: Charles II enters London, marking the Restoration of the Monarchy on his 30th birthday.
Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay become the first people to reach the summit of Mount Everest as part of a British expedition.
The birth of John F. Kennedy; his visit to London as President was a major political event.
Joan of Arc is burned at the stake by the English in Rouen.
The RAF launches the first 'thousand-bomber raid' against the city of Cologne.
The death of English actor and comedian Peter Sellers.

The Dovecote Restoration: Work is completed on the restoration of the 18th-century timber-framed dovecote under the direction of Professor Richardson. Though later demolished in 1966, this restoration was a major local heritage effort.
The Battle of Jutland, the largest naval battle of World War I, begins between the British and German fleets.
The Great Clock at the Palace of Westminster, containing Big Ben, begins to keep time.
Waltheof, Earl of Northumbria, is executed, the last English earl to survive the Norman Conquest.
